data.title: SPY: A V-Shape Rebound Pushes Valuation Back To The Unsustainable Level (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A V-Shape Rebound

The S&P 500 index (SP500, NYSEARCA:SPY) has rallied more than 20% from its intraday low near 4800 on April 7. Many institutional investors, including hedge funds that were previously net short, were caught off guard. So, what changed over the past two months?

Simply put, the equity selloff was triggered by President Trump's tariff policy, which led markets to price in a stagflation outlook in late 2025. I published an article on March 31, just before the "Liberation Day". I noted, "Based on Trump's pro-growth and deregulation agenda, I believe a Trump Put is very likely. This can increase the probability of a V-shaped recovery."

Just hours after implementing reciprocal tariffs on April 2, Trump delayed most of them by 90 days. Both bond and equity markets were signaling that the economy was going to crash. At the same time, when the S&P had corrected 10%, I cautioned that we were not out of the woods yet and issued a Hold rating on SPY, suggesting that a potential buying opportunity could emerge if the selloff reached bear market levels which was considered a 20% drawdown.

So how could SPY bounce back toward all-time highs so fast? I believe there are four main factors behind the rally. First and foremost obviously, is the de-escalation in global trade tensions, particularly between the U.S and China. Second, economic data shows resilience, especially the job market. Third, the 1Q FY2025 earnings season came in strong, driven by still robust AI demand, which benefited the index heavyweight Magnificent 7 and helped offset weakness in trade sensitive companies. Lastly, I believe FOMO and short coverings have pushed the index higher to the current levels, as the near-term price actions are largely driven by sentiment.

I'm Bearish on the Equity Market in 2H 2025

So now what? I'll give you three reasons to consider selling into the rally. First, the de-escalation in trade tensions doesn't mean the issue is completely resolved. Ongoing trade talks could drag on for years before reaching any meaningful agreements. As long as the current administration insists on so-called "fair-trade" agreements. This week, we saw the U.S and China reach a deal addressing previously unsettled terms, which looks more like a truce. Moreover, Trump announced that he will impose unilateral tariffs on most trade partners in two weeks, before the postponed July deadline, which adds fresh uncertainty to the market. With SPY trading nearly all-time highs and the VIX dropping to 17 (from over 50 in April), the market has priced in an assumption that major trade disruptions are off the table, which looks too optimistic to me. We can see the equity risk premium has been trending lower as the S&P 500 rebounds.

Second, the economic and corporate earnings data may take several more months to reflect the initial tariff impacts. Many companies saw a temporary boost from a tariff pull forward demand that inflated a strong earnings result in the recent earnings season. In addition, a potential recession could also dampen AI infrastructure spending in the near term. Lastly, we should not overlook the risk of a growing fiscal deficit, which could push long end rates higher (5% for +20-year treasuries), trigger a bond vigilante response (higher yields in bonds make equities less attractive), and increase the risk of further credit rating downgrades on U.S sovereign debt.

Third, while momentum is also a key factor that has fueled this rebound, valuation multiples are stretched again as earnings estimates for S&P 500 composites continue to be revised lower for FY2025. This disconnect adds more downside risk. Meanwhile, the U.S dollar is down 9% YTD, indicating more outflows from the U.S assets. That said, I've downgraded SPY from Hold to Sell.

Earnings Results in S&P 500 are Lifted by the Magnificent 7

According to a FactSet report, S&P 500 earnings growth for 1Q FY2025 was largely driven by the Magnificent 7. The actual growth contribution turned out to be much larger than what the market was expecting three months ago, before 1Q FY2025 earnings season started. As shown on the chart, the Magnificent 7 delivered 27.7% YoY in earnings, up from a previous consensus of 16%. In contrast, earnings growth for the other 493 companies in the S&P 500 were largely muted. Another factor is that the equal weighted S&P 500 index has barely recovered from the April 7 low, while the S&P 500 cap weighted index is approaching a new all-time high. This price divergence suggests an ongoing AI froth in the market, pushing valuations higher.

Now, take a look at the chart above. Consensus for the S&P 500 in CY2025 has been cutting lower over the past few months, down to around $265 per share. At the recent $6000 price level on the index, that pushes the P/E CY2025 fwd to 22.6x, which is well above both the 5-year and 10-year averages, as shown below.

The chart shows where the S&P 500's forward P/E landed during the April selloff. When the index dipped towards 4800 level, the multiple briefly fell below the 10-year average of 18.3x. Now, the P/E has climbed to 22.6x, approaching the high end of the range. We need to ask: is this rally starting to run ahead of the earnings growth again?

How Trump’s Immigration Policies Are Reshaping the U.S. Labor Market

While recent economic data continues to send mixed signals, I think we may see a further slowdown in 2H CY2025. Last week's jobs report showed the unemployment rate in May holding steady at 4.2%, in-line with the previous month. However, Initial Jobless Claims jumped higher. In addition, despite cooling CPI data this week, we could see a potential rebound in the following months, which may keep the Fed on hold a little bit longer. This could limit any further expansion in S&P 500's valuations.

We also saw a recent escalation in Trump's immigration policy, as he doubled down on massive deportations of illegal immigrants, reversing Biden's more softened immigration policy that had helped a surge in labor force since the pandemic. If this crackdown continues, it could backfire on the U.S labor market and trigger ripple effects in the economy.

That said, front-loaded corporate spending and resilient AI demand helped power strong 1Q FY2025 earnings. However, the future trade uncertainty, lagging impact on economic data, and a Fed that doesn't seem to be in a rush to ease are the risks we need to focus on. Without an AI boost, it's hard to justify further upside in the S&P 500 from here. Sure, CTAs and FOMO-driven sentiment could pump the price to new all-time highs in the near term. But I'm questioning if the rally is actually sustainable.

What Could Go Wrong?

Since much of the S&P 500's valuation expansion is related to AI-driven growth, we will need to see a continued strength in AI investments through 2025 and 2026 to justify the current multiple levels. According to UBS research, global AI capex is expected to grow by 60% YoY this year, followed by another 33% YoY in 2026. We also notice that these hyperscalers are raising their AI spending outlook for this year and reluctant to factor in any near-term macro uncertainty into their full-year outlooks. This also can explain why the SOXX ETF (NASDAQ:SOXX) has outperformed both SPY and QQQ since the April rally. The premium multiple on the S&P 500 index can stay elevated as long as mega cap earnings are not significantly hurt by a slowdown in AI spending. Otherwise, we would see a multiple contraction in valuations.

Conclusion

The bottom line: SPY achieved another V-shaped recovery as trade tensions faded, and the market priced out the stagflation risk in the near term. Still, I think the rally looks too fast and premature, considering trade uncertainty remains and the lagging impact of economic data. The biggest driver holding up the S&P 500's premium valuation is AI demand outlook which is expected to provide strong support by current robust AI spending. But from here, I see limited upside. Therefore, I am downgrading SPY to a Sell.

data.title: PGIM Select Real Estate Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

NORTH AMERICA

We continue to view the U.S. real estate investment trust ('REIT') market as attractive, trading at an approximate 5% discount to NAV, compared to a long-term average of flat. We expect accelerating earnings growth in both 2025 and 2026 (roughly 6.3% and 6.7% respectively). In addition, the funds from operations (FFO) multiple spread of the S&P 500 vs. REITs is the largest since the financial crisis.

Given this attractive valuation and improving fundamentals, we wouldn’t be surprised to see increased private equity activity in the REIT market in 2025. Last year, the REIT market witnessed three privatizations with takeover prices represented 25-30% premium to the stocks’ prior day’s close. With deals across the apartments, single-family rental, and retail sectors.

In terms of positioning, we remain overweight senior housing. Similar to data centers, we continue to see a strong, multi-year runway of growth based on secular demand trends and limited supply. We’ve added to our overweight in cold storage given attractive valuation support, defensive demand base (food consumption), and improving operating efficiencies. We’ve trimmed our overweight to the mall sector given recent outperformance and added to apartments. Apartment REITs are likely to see accelerating rental rate growth over the next several years and still trade at attractive valuation relative to their portfolio’s private value. We are balanced on the office sector, given discounted valuations but some persistent headwinds. That said, increased volatility has allowed for tactical opportunities, especially in markets with unique demand dynamics, such as New York City.

EUROPE

The European market remains at wide discounts to its historical average valuation metrics following its poor absolute and relative performance in 2024 despite a modestly positive start to 2025. These valuations reflect a combination of a slower economic growth outlook for the region, political risk and higher average levels of financial leverage. The growth outlook in Europe has been revised down and is expected to remain subdued in 2025 and possibly into 2026. Trade disruption from the announcement of U.S. tariffs on all global trading partners introduces additional downside risks to growth, especially in those countries in Europe more dependent on exports to the U.S. E.U. exporters are facing a 20% tariff on exports to the U.S. and the U.K. tariff has been set at 10%. The announcement of these tariffs on April 2nd caused 10- year bond yields to plummet and saw a significant bounce in the European listed real estate index on the day. Weakening bond yields reflected a worsening economic growth outlook across the region and expectations for further central bank interest rate cuts this year from both the ECB and BOE.

Despite the prevailing macro uncertainty, we see a moderately positive outlook on balance for the listed real estate market in Europe. Private market real estate values are around trough levels in most sectors and countries and public market share prices offer discounts to these private market valuations. Many companies offer attractive cash flow yields at present. Expected further falls in interest rates are supportive for the companies in the region that carry higher leverage. Finally, occupier market fundamentals are positive in the structurally growing sectors and new supply is generally under control.

However, we are aware of the risks for real estate of further economic slowing in the region. To manage this risk, our investment focus in 2025 in Europe is on the sectors with structural growth and positive occupier trends: industrial/logistics, data centers, selected multifamily residential, student accommodation and self-storage. We also retain an overweight to selected retail names where consumption has proved to be resilient and wider cap rates offer opportunities. We remain cautious overall and underweight on the office market due to higher vacancy in secondary locations, substantial capex backlogs and the potential for further private market value adjustments.

We are broadly neutral on the European region due to the economic growth challenges and political risks facing the region. However, the region’s significant public market underperformance in 2024 does mean that many companies are offering attractive relative valuation opportunities, and we will look to make new investments or increase positions in companies where we see catalysts and have conviction on earnings growth and/or exceptional value opportunities.

ASIA PACIFIC

As a consequence of growth concerns emanating from rising global trade tensions, the market recession risk has risen to the extent it is now pricing in 3 further cuts by the Federal Reserve by year-end. Rising recession risk has caused an attendant decline in sovereign bond yields with the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield down 37bp to 4.2% and Singapore 10-year bond yield down 25bps to 2.6%. This presents a favorable backdrop for REITs in general and especially for names underpinned by strong structural factors that should outperform in a mild inflationary environment. In Asia, the Bank of Japan is on an opposite track from the Fed as it seeks to normalize monetary policy and raise interest rates. We are underweight the Japanese developers given limited remaining upside from shareholder reform policies. We are overweight the JREITs given the relative stability and lower start-point of Japanese interest rates with our preference tilted towards the hospitality names. In Australia, we are positive on residential and retail REITs that benefit from domestic consumption and declining interest rates. For SREITs, our preference is with resilient industrial / data center REITs with solid dividend growth and low vacancy. We are neutral in Hong Kong and prefer non-discretionary retail REITs that benefit from lower yields and the potential REIT-Connect arrangement with China.

QUARTER

The Fund (MUTF:SREAX) underperformed the benchmark, the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Index, for the first quarter. Stock selection was negative, and asset allocation was neutral.

North America region lagged. An overweight position in data centers was the main driver of the underperformance. Additionally, stock selection was weak in the triple net and office sectors. Lastly, an underweight to gaming securities dragged on performance. To the positive, stock selection was favorable in the residential and shopping center sectors as well as in Canada.

Europe detracted this quarter. Although stock selection in France was positive, weak security selection in Belgium and an underweighting to Switzerland dragged on relative performance.

The Asia Pacific region underperformed for the period. Stock selection was negative in Japan, Australia and Singapore.

data.title: PGIM Real Assets Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

The second Trump administration hit the ground running in Q1 2025, advancing aggressive policy initiatives on tariffs, immigration, executive branch reform, and cease-fires in Israel and Ukraine. Tariff policies have caused significant disruption to the economic landscape. Central bank responses to tariffs are critically important; if mishandled, tariffs could lead to higher prices and dampen long-term economic growth. However, accurately gauging tariff impacts amid other economic variables is difficult, raising the risk of policy errors. The Federal Reserve (Fed) has adopted a “wait-and-see” approach, but futures markets are pricing in additional rate cuts to counteract the expected effects of tariffs. Consumer sentiment has declined, and inflation expectations have risen, reflecting the strain of the new trade policies. Although underlying economic indicators remain solid, as noted by Chair Powell, the Q1 Atlanta Gross Domestic Product Now (GDPNow) forecast shows a sharp decline, signaling a potential economic slowdown. Although a recession is not yet evident in the data, the risk of a recession occurring over the next year has increased, especially if trade tensions escalate and higher tariffs are implemented.

Meanwhile, the Eurozone economy shows signs of weakness. Industrial production is struggling amid headwinds from high input costs and rising foreign competition. However, increased military spending by North Atlantic Treaty Organization ('NATO') nations could provide a stimulative economic boost, even as the European Central Bank (ECB) continues cutting rates. Japan’s economy, while still growing, faces persistent inflation concerns, prompting the Bank of Japan to raise interest rates. China’s economy has achieved its growth target through stimulus measures, but the challenging external economic environment poses significant risks to sustaining this momentum.

U.S. equities posted negative performance in the first quarter of 2025, with the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) returning -4.3%. Despite positive returns from seven out of eleven sectors in Q1, sharp losses in Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary drove the market decline, while Energy emerged as the top performer. The Q1 market pullback has improved U.S. equity valuations, with the forward price-to-earnings multiple on the S&P 500 easing to around 21x, down from nearly 25x at the start of the year. However, the risks of policy missteps and geopolitical shocks persist. Corporate management sentiment remains optimistic, and earnings expectations for U.S. large-caps are strong. In contrast, small-cap earnings face potential downward revisions if the economic outlook deteriorates.

The risk-return tradeoff between stocks and bonds is mixed, with positive equity-bond correlations diminishing diversification benefits and a less favorable yield gap between bonds and stocks. For the first quarter of 2025, large-caps (S&P 500: -4.3% and Russell 1000: -4.5%) outperformed small-caps (Russell 2000: -9.5%) but underperformed mid-caps (Russell Mid-Cap: -3.4%). In terms of style, value stocks (Russell 1000 Value: 2.1%) performed significantly better than growth stocks (Russell 1000 Growth: -10.0%). Over the past year, large-caps (S&P 500: 8.3% and Russell 1000: 7.8%) and mid-caps (Russell Mid-Cap: 2.6%) have gained while small-caps (Russell 2000: -4.0%) have experienced negative performance. Moreover, growth stocks (Russell 1000 Growth: 7.8%) have slightly outperformed value stocks (Russell 1000 Value: 7.2%).

International equity markets rose in Q1 of 2025, with developed and emerging markets posting respective total returns of 6.9% and 2.9% in U.S. dollar terms. Developed markets saw broad-based gains, with a particularly strong quarter in Europe, where Spain, Germany, and Italy each recorded returns of more than 12.0%. On the emerging markets side, positive returns were driven by Poland and Brazil. Recent months have seen rotations across asset classes. U.S. markets have lagged due to growth concerns, while international equities and emerging markets have benefited amid improved growth prospects and easing tariff fears. The dominance of growth stocks has diminished, reflecting changing market dynamics. While U.S. equity valuations have slightly improved, international equities, particularly in emerging markets, offer more attractive valuations. If trade tensions ease and global financial conditions improve, the rotation toward these markets could gain momentum.

Against this backdrop, the MSCI Europe, Australasia, and Far East (EAFE) Index posted a total return of 6.9% for the first quarter and a 12-month return of 4.9%, with value (12.8%) performing markedly better than growth (-2.6%) in the past 12 months. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index delivered net returns of 2.9% for Q1 and 8.1% over the past year.

The broad U.S. fixed income index delivered a positive return of 2.8% in Q1, building on the upward momentum from the end of 2024. Increased volatility across asset classes is being driven by trade policy uncertainty, fluctuating growth and inflation expectations, and central bank actions. With U.S. debt surpassing $36 trillion, proposed tax cuts and spending plans have raised concerns about fiscal sustainability and bond market stability. Without offsetting spending cuts, rising deficits could put upward pressure on interest rates and potentially jeopardize the dollar’s status as the global reserve currency.

Nonetheless, total return expectations for high yield bonds are solid, supported by higher yields, even as the growth environment remains benign. For the quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, bonds returned 2.8%. Treasuries and Investment Grade Corporate Credit ('IG') also performed well, returning 2.9% and 2.3%, respectively, while High Yield Corporate ('HY') bonds slightly underperformed with a gain of 1.0%. Long-dated bonds, in both corporates and Treasuries, outperformed their short-term sector counterparts. Over the past year, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index has returned 4.9%, Treasuries gained 4.5%, and U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protects Securities ('TIPS') increased 6.2%. In the corporate bond space, IG and HY posted gains of 4.9% and 7.7%, respectively, over the same period.

International fixed income markets also delivered positive returns in the first quarter of 2025. The U.S. dollar weakened against all developed market currencies during the quarter, with particularly sharp declines against the Swedish krona and Norwegian krone. As a result, hedged investors generally experience lower returns compared to their unhedged counterparts. Despite core inflation remaining modestly above 2%, the ECB has continued to steer policy rates toward neutral. During the first quarter of 2025 alone, the ECB has cut policy rates 50 basis points (bps), following a 100bps reduction in 2024, bringing the deposit rate to 2.5%. Meanwhile, the Bank of Japan left policy rates unchanged in Q4 of 2024 but kicked off 2025 with a rate hike in January. For the quarter, the Bloomberg Unhedged Global Aggregate Index gained 2.6%, while the Bloomberg Unhedged Emerging Market Aggregate Index returned 2.3%. Over the past year, the Bloomberg Unhedged Global Aggregate Index delivered a 3.0% return, underperforming the Bloomberg Unhedged Emerging Market Aggregate Index which gained 7.4%.

QUARTER

In the first quarter, the PGIM Real Assets Fund underperformed its blended benchmark, but outperformed the Bloomberg Barclays U.S. TIPS primary benchmark. Asset allocation contributed positively to relative outperformance versus the blended benchmark. Underlying managers detracted value during the quarter.

Asset allocation contributed positively to the Fund’s performance. Off-benchmark exposure to energy infrastructure and gold were a positive contributor as it outperformed the blended benchmark. The underweight in Real Estate and TIPS also contributed positively to the Fund’s performance. The underweight commodities detracted value as it was the strongest gainer over the quarter. Off-benchmark exposure to global infrastructure was a modest negative and cash detracted from performance over the quarter.

Manager allocation impact was negative for the quarter. Almost all the managers underperformed their benchmarks over the quarter. The PGIM Select Real Estate Fund and the PGIM TIPS fund underperformed their benchmarks by a significant margin, while the PGIM Quant Solutions Commodity Strategies underperformed its benchmark by the smallest margin.

data.title: Why I Am Maintaining My Buy Rating For Broadridge Financial
data.description:
Investment thesis

Broadridge Financial Solutions, Inc. (NYSE:BR) is a solid company with a history of delivering higher share prices, capital gains, and dividends. Yet, it received just a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant system on Tuesday, June 11, 2025:

In this article, I explore the reasons for the Quant's Hold rating and contrast it with my Buy rating.

About Broadridge

The company calls itself a "global financial technology leader" in its 10-K for 2024. It went on to explain that it delivers technology-driven solutions to three end markets: investing, corporate governance, and communications.

These technologies allow banks, broker-dealers, asset and wealth managers, as well as public companies and others to "operate, innovate and grow."

It operates through two segments: (1) Investor Communication Solutions and (2) Global Technology and Operations.

At the close on June 11, 2025, its shares traded at $241.18, and it had a market cap of $28.54 billion.

Most recent results

To put the current results in context, this chart shows revenue, net income, basic EPS and normalized diluted EPS over the past decade:

Note how steadily revenue has grown every year since 2018. Net income and earnings haven't grown as steadily, but over the years here, they have kept up with revenue growth. This is a solid record indicating the company is well managed and able to stay on its growth path.

As the right side of the chart shows, Broadridge posted another excellent quarter.

Its Q3 fiscal 2025 results, for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, were released on May 1. Key results included:

  • Recurring revenues grew by 7.0% (8.0% in constant currency).
  • Diluted EPS increased 15.0% to $2.05, while adjusted EPS went up 9.0% to $2.44.
  • And management reaffirmed its fiscal-year 2025 guidance of 6.0% to 8.0% in recurring revenue growth. They also forecast adjusted EBITDA growth to be about 10.0%.

CEO Tim Gokey said, "Our ability to deliver strong results in the face of increased market uncertainty highlights the strength and stability of our business model, and Broadridge is well-positioned to deliver another year of steady and consistent growth in fiscal 2025."

His comments are backed up by a net income margin of 11.65%, which is 87% higher than the Industrials sector median. Similarly, its return on common equity of 33.92% far outpaces the sector median of 12.50%.

Broadridge's Quant ratings

Despite these robust results, the Quant system has only a Hold rating, but it also has rated Broadridge a Buy and (briefly) a Strong Buy in the past year:

On the right side of this chart, we see the share price scale, ranging from $175 to $250. That's relevant to our discussion because it turns out the Quant rating is quite sensitive to price changes.

For example, on September 18, 2024, the share price inched down from $210.96 to $208.59, a difference of $2.37 or 0.01%. It seems that was enough to push the Quant score up from 3.08 to 3.69, and switch from a Hold to a Buy rating. All other criteria-valuation, growth, profitability, momentum and EPS revisions-stayed the same.

The Buy rating held until November 7, when the price increased from $219.46 to $221.18. The Quant score then fell from 3.92 to 3.03, putting it back into Hold territory, as well as improving its valuation grade, reducing its momentum score, and downgrading its EPS revisions grade:

Note that valuation and momentum are directly tied to the share price.

Here's another example: The Broadridge rating shifted from Hold to Buy on March 27, 2025. In this case, the price increased $0.70 while nothing else changed. However, that was enough to make the score jump from 3.25 to 4.17:

And here is the most recent transition: on April 1, 2025, Buy turned into a Hold, as the price increased by $2.26. That was enough to lower the valuation grade from D to D-minus, and improve momentum from an A-minus to an A. Of course, the Quant score also fell:

Broadridge has remained a Quant Hold since that date.

In at least eight of the 13 transitions that occurred since September 18, price was the obvious main driver. In the five cases where it was not, some were because of other price-driven factors, including valuation and momentum.

Our first takeaway is that the Quant system is sensitive to share prices, with a change of less than 1.0% capable of changing the rating.

This Quant characteristic is in line with value investing principles, in which lower entry prices are likely to produce superior returns over the medium and long terms. In the short term, gains and losses tend to reflect chance or volatility, rather than strategy.

Buying at a lower price, even if by only a couple of dollars, also produces higher dividends. Broadridge currently pays a modest dividend, so this could make a difference in the long term. And even small dividends or differences in them can make a difference, as demonstrated in this chart of Broadridge's price return and total return:

A second takeaway involves earnings revisions, which I sometimes find at odds with Wall Street ratings and price forecasts.

For example, on Wednesday, June 11, there were seven Down revisions and just one Up revision. Alternatively, that means 87.0% are Down while just 13.0% are Up. Therefore, one might expect Sell recommendations to prevail among the analysts, yet that is not the case:

This bullishness also holds in their EPS estimates for the next five years, with forecasts clustered around 10.0% per year gains:

Further, the analysts' average one-year price target is $256.27, a 5.46% increase over the past year. That's also not in line with 87% of analysts' revisions.

Now, EPS revisions do not appear to play as significant a role as price in the Quant system, but it is worth keeping in mind if these ratings play an important role in your investing decisions.

Overall, the Quant ratings are worth heeding, especially if you are a value investor, or you want an edge when it comes to returns. Purchasing during a Quant Buy rating likely increases the odds of higher capital gains and dividends.

Why I'm sticking with my Buy rating

Despite that Hold rating, I'm sticking with the Buy rating I posted in my November 10, 2024 article, Broadridge: More High Single-Digit Earnings Growth Ahead.

If you look at a 10-year chart of Broadridge's share price, you see it rising quite steadily, albeit with some volatility:

While there is too much fluctuation to call this a bond substitute, it is significantly less volatile than the S&P 500, which has a Beta of 1.0. Broadridge has a 24-month Beta of 0.62, which means its systemic risk is 38.0% less than that of the market as a whole.

Both the company and the Wall Street analysts expect more growth this year. Management's guidance is for recurring revenue growth of 6.0% to 8.0%. According to the Q3 earnings call, recurring revenue represents 94.0% of its total revenue, so non-recurring revenue is unlikely to change this range by much.

As we saw above, Wall Street analysts expect the company to turn that revenue increase into normalized EPS growth of 10.09% in fiscal 2025 (ending June 30 this year), and 9.35% in fiscal 2026.

It's also worth noting that Broadridge is growing its normalized EPS more quickly than its revenue, which is a sign of growing productivity. In turn, that means the company is increasingly squeezing more earnings out of each dollar of revenue. Here are the specifics for the next three years:

  • Fiscal 2025: revenue growth 5.97%, EPS growth 10.09%.
  • Fiscal 2026: revenue growth 4.95%, EPS growth 9.35%.
  • Fiscal 2027: revenue growth 5.44%, EPS growth 10.05%.

Investors should also find encouragement in the fact Broadridge had a revenue backlog of $450 million at the end of Q3 (revenue or bookings that have not been recognized).

In a nutshell, Broadridge is a growing company in a growing industry, and an industry that is undergoing extensive changes because of new technologies, the most important of which may be artificial intelligence. According to a report by Benchmark International, the global financial services industry is expected to grow by an average of 7.6% [CAGR] per year between now and 2028.

So, I expect Broadridge to keep growing, much as it has done in the past. Of course, if the American or global economies go into recession, those growth figures may need to be scaled back.

Assuming that growth, I think it makes sense to enter now rather than waiting for the next Buy signal because that signal can be triggered by even a very small price change.

Valuation

With its growing share price, it is not surprising that the Seeking Alpha system gives it a low grade, D, for valuation.

Given Broadridge's history, I'm not sure there will be any point in the next few years when it gets a good mark for valuation. This is one of those stocks that demands you look ahead, rather than back. Of course, if you can pick it up for a few dollars less, as shown by the Quant ratings, that would be even better.

To set a one-year valuation target, I will base it on the estimated earnings growth posted by the Wall Street analysts. As this chart of price, basic EPS, and normalized EPS shows, normalized diluted EPS has been a good predictor of prices in the past:

As you will recall, the Wall Street analysts forecast growth of 10.09% for fiscal 2025 and 9.35% for fiscal 2026. Since there is one quarter remaining in fiscal 2025, I will take 25% of 10.09%, which is 2.52%. And 75% of fiscal 2025's 9.35% is 7.01%.

Adding these two results gives us a total of 9.53%, and adding that much to the June 11 close of $241.18 gives us a target of $264.16.

That's greater than the average estimates of the Wall Street analysts, who see one-year growth of 5.46% and a price of $256.17.

As noted, I have a Buy rating and there have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days. The Quant system has a Hold, while five Wall Street analysts have Buys and another five have Hold recommendations.

Risk factors

Because Broadridge is a financial services company, it works with large volumes of personal information, intellectual property, and other sensitive information. Investors, therefore, should be concerned about potential data breaches.

It operates in highly competitive markets, and competitors include large firms such as SS&C Technologies Holdings, Inc. (SSNC), Genpact Limited (G), and ExlService Holdings, Inc. (EXLS).

Financial services companies such as Broadridge face extensive regulation, and failure to comply could compromise its financial health and reputation.

Significant changes in American or global politics or economic conditions could materially reduce its revenue and earnings. In its 10-K for fiscal 2024, it lists many potential hazards including legislative/regulatory changes, social/health conditions such as COVID-19, and the level or volatility of interest rates.

My assumptions, as well as those of the company and analysts, are based on a continuation of conditions in the recent past. If our assumptions turn out to be incorrect, our forecasts will be less accurate or invalid.

Conclusion

I am maintaining my Buy rating for Broadridge Financial Solutions, Inc. because I expect its history of making higher capital gains and dividend payments to continue.

While I respect the Quant system's Hold rating, I will cite the old investing adage that time in the market beats timing the market. In this case, the current Hold would mean waiting for a price reduction that warrants a Buy rating (value investors may wish to ignore this advice).

data.title: PGIM Jennison Utility Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

On the whole, U.S. utility stocks performed well during 1Q25 relative to broad U.S. equity indices as several factors, including rising concerns about the Trump administration’s escalating tariff policies causing a potential recession in the U.S., contributed to investors rotating into securities such as U.S. Treasuries and defensive equities such as U.S. regulated utilities, particularly those whose performance had lagged during 2024.

After a strong start to the year, power generation companies as well as data center stocks performed relatively poorly during 1Q25 as DEEPSEEK, a Chinese artificial intelligence company, triggered a meaningful selloff in AI-related stocks. In January 2025, DeepSeek unveiled an innovative reasoning model that raised investor concerns that DeepSeek’s reportedly low-cost approach could significantly reduce demand for the most advanced AI hardware and, by extension, reduce demand for highly electricity-consumptive data center capacity. This sharp reversal in investor sentiment regarding AI-related stocks served as a strong headwind for U.S.-based power generators and data center companies who had rallied throughout 2024 as key beneficiaries of strong demand due to accelerating AI-related capital expenditure (capex).

QUARTER

The PGIM Jennison Utility Fund (MUTF:PRUAX) advanced but underperformed the 4.9% return of the S&P 500 Utilities Index (the Index) over the first quarter.

In absolute terms, utilities overall were positive for the quarter, led by multi-utilities and electric utilities. The independent power producers (IPPs) & renewables segment resulted in detracting from performance this quarter. Gas utilities contributed marginally to the Fund, while select midstream energy names also had small contributions to performance.

Relative to the Index, utilities detracted the most from performance this quarter. Within the Utilities sector, electric utilities were the largest detractors, due to a relative underweight. In addition, a relative overweight in IPPs and non-index exposure to select names in multi-utilities and water utilities further detracted from performance. Out-of-index exposure to midstream energy names detracted marginally for the period.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • Entergy Corporation (ETR)
  • Exelon Corporation (EXC)
  • CenterPoint Energy (CNP)

Entergy Corporation (ETR), together with its subsidiaries, engages in the production and retail distribution of electricity in the U.S. ETR continued to outperform its regulated utility peers in 1Q25 after announcing additional datacenter deals (following the 4Q24 deal w/ Meta) that further pads the company’s long-term earnings growth projections above the industry average. The team remains positive on ETR that stands to benefit from continued AI datacenter buildout while supported by constructive regulated ratemaking structures.

Exelon Corporation (EXC) is a utility services holding company. It operates six transmission & distribution only utilities serving six states (IL, MD, PA, DE, D.C., NJ). EXC’s shares rebounded this quarter as a benign Illinois rate case outcome in December 2024 improved investor sentiment on the state’s regulatory environment, helping the stock to narrow the relative valuation discount. We have since added to the Fund’s position and are overweight the name relative to the benchmark; the team believes EXC valuations will continue to improve over time.

CenterPoint Energy (CNP) engages in the business of power generation and distribution. It operates through the following segments: Electric, Corporate and Other, and Natural Gas. CNP shares continued to outperform this quarter after recovering from the Hurricane Beryl mishap in July 2024. Management has taken positive steps to improve legislative/regulatory relationships, including completing the Houston electric rate case constructively. Despite shares now trading at a price to earnings (P/E) premium relative to regulated utility peers, we see further earnings per share (EPS) upside to its current 6-8% guidance that should warrant CNP deserving a best-in-class multiple in the space.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • Sempra (SRE)
  • PG&E (PCG)
  • Vistra Corp. (VST)

Sempra (SRE) operates as an energy infrastructure company in the United States and internationally. It operates through three segments: Sempra California, Sempra Texas Utilities, and Sempra Infrastructure. Shares meaningfully underperformed in 1Q25 as management meaningfully reduced its forward EPS guidance citing various factors, including rate recovery lag in Texas, higher financing costs, and less favorable rate case outcome in California. This update came as a surprise, especially given management’s enthusiasm (as recently as late 2024) on robust Texas growth potentially accelerating its earnings growth. The team moderated the Fund’s position following the update, now relatively neutral to the benchmark. While PGIM believes it may take time for the company to regain credibility, the team is encouraged by management’s quick actions in recent weeks to reduce its non-utility asset exposure and improve the balance sheet. We look for additional strategic updates in the coming months that could further point the company in the right direction.

PG&E (PCG) is a San Francisco, CA, based energy holding company that generates, transmits and distributes electricity. The company produces power using nuclear, hydro, fuel cell, solar, and fossil fuel sources, while serving customers through its main operating subsidiary, Pacific Gas and Electric (PG&E). PCG shares underperformed in January as the Los Angeles fires shook investor confidence regarding California wildfire risk, despite the fires not occurring near PG&E’s service territory and with financial protections in place per the 2019 legislation. While the team believes PCG’s wildfire mitigation investments have proved effective in northern California, and the industry seems to have a constructive relationship with the state government, the regulatory/legislative process to further enhance protections for investor-owned utilities will take time and will likely be volatile. As such the team has hesitated to take advantage of what we would otherwise consider to be an inordinate sell-off.

Vistra Corp. (VST) is an independent power producer operating an integrated retail and electric power generation business primarily in markets throughout the U.S.

After performing well in 2024, IPPs such as VST underperformed in 1Q25 as delays in AI datacenter contract announcements and regulatory uncertainty in Texas and mid-Atlantic region impacted investor enthusiasm. We still remain positive on IPPs as we expect power deals to be announced in 2H2025 once regulatory clarity emerges, along with positive power price moves that should elevate forward year earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisaion (EBITDA), all not reflected in current valuations. That said, given recent macro volatility, we reduced the Fund’s exposure to VST and other IPPs for risk management.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

PGIM believes our electric utility, power generation, and renewable energy holdings are well-positioned to capitalize upon a projected upward inflection in electricity demand growth driven by rapidly rising data center power needs, manufacturing/industrial re-shoring, continued electric vehicle adoption, and further electrification of a broad array of industries. Based on PGIM’s channel checks with AI industry participants, the team believes the selloff in U.S. power generator stocks triggered by the unveiling of China’s DeepSeek AI reasoning model in January 2025 was overdone; the outlook for AI-related infrastructure capex continues to look very robust and we anticipate a reduction in regulatory and legislative uncertainty in 2H25 regarding colocation of large data centers with power plants and transmission cost allocation could serve as a positive catalyst for U.S. power generator stocks. Additionally, we view increased focus on electric grid reliability and resiliency from regulators and policymakers as supportive for sustaining robust levels of regulated rate-base investment which we anticipate will further support attractive EPS growth for most regulated electric utilities in the U.S. and Western Europe through 2030 and likely beyond.

In PGIM’s view, North American natural gas infrastructure will play a critical role in supplying the new gas-fired power plants that will likely be required to serve the power demand growth dramatically accelerating through 2030 and beyond; we believe several new proposed natural gas pipeline projects announced in 2H24 and early 2025 corroborate this optimistic outlook. We remain selective with the Fund’s energy infrastructure exposure with a focus on strategic natural gas infrastructure networks, strong operational and financial execution that translates into attractive levels of free cash flow, and a healthy return of capital to shareholders through dividend increases and/or share repurchases.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Natural Resources Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

Following a January recovery for most Natural Resources commodities, and PGIM Jennison Natural Resources Fund (MUTF:PGNAX), after the late-2024 swoon, general economic concerns centering on tariff proposals by the Trump Administration led to a reversal mid-quarter. Reflecting such economic and geopolitical uncertainty, gold performed best among commodities, followed by natural gas and copper, although the copper equities failed to follow. Gold past the record $3,000/oz milestone late in the quarter amid inflation and trade war potential.

QUARTER

The Fund advanced but underperformed its Lipper Global Natural Resources Index (the Index) benchmark, which gained 4.4% for the month.

In absolute terms, the Fund’s gains were driven by its weighting and performance in energy stocks-including both energy exploration & production companies as well as energy services & equipment names. However, the Fund’s holdings in renewables-related stocks saw declines for the quarter.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM)
  • Shell PLC (SHEL)
  • EQT Corp (EQT)

Agnico Eagle Mines is a Canadian gold mining company, producing precious metals from operations in Canada, Australia, Finland, and Mexico. Agnico Eagle Mines has benefited from the rising gold price and declining input costs. The large, liquid producer with a lower-risk asset base delivered a largely in-line quarter with an attractive longer-term outlook that held together. Agnico’s balance sheet should clean up quickly at current gold prices, making way for accelerated share buybacks.

Shell PLC operates as an energy and petrochemical company in Europe, Asia, Oceania, Africa, the United States, and the rest of the Americas. The company operates through Integrated Gas, Upstream, Marketing, Chemicals and Products, and Renewables and Energy Solutions segments. As the leading global liquiefied natural gas (LNG) supplier, Shell benefitted from rising U.S. and European natural gas prices through the quarter. Additionally, a late-quarter analyst day reflected early delivery of a profitability-growth plan and lent credence to newly established three-year ambitions.

EQT Corp operates as a natural gas production company in the United States. The company sells natural gas and natural gas liquids to marketers, utilities, and industrial customers through pipelines located in the Appalachian Basin. The company delivered strong fourth quarter 2024 results, in turn validating its mid-summer midstream acquisition that, by lowering its cost base and improving gas-price realizations, bolsters its free-cash flow outlook. Having paid down a significant amount of acquisition-related debt that free cash should largely accrue directly to shareholders.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • Ivanhoe Mines (OTCQX:IVPAF)
  • Cameco (CCJ)
  • Chart Industries (GTLS)

Ivanhoe Mines explores for and develops mineral properties. The company produces copper from a mine in the Democratic Republic of Congo and is also developing a zinc-copper mine. The company continued to struggle receiving consistent power to its operations which had a compounding impact on volumes and per unit costs as the company had to use higher cost off grid power. In addition, unrest in the eastern part of the country along the border of Rwanda, has weighed on sentiment as concerns have arisen over the stability of the government. While we continue to monitor the unrest and potential impact on operations, we believe the operational and power issues the company has faced over the last several quarters are dissipating and expect to see improved performance in the back half of the year.

Cameco explores, develops, mines, refines, converts, and fabricates uranium. The company offers uranium for sale as fuel to nuclear power reactors worldwide. There is a growing appreciation for nuclear energy’s zero carbon, extremely reliable characteristics to be part of the solution when it comes to the lowering of emissions in concert with growing world economies. While the company continues to execute well and fourth quarter results were positive, given announced deals by hyper-scalers using nuclear to power data centers, uranium equities got caught up in the Deepseek artificial intelligence (AI)/data center unwind experienced by the market in the first quarter. In addition, with uncertainty around tariffs there has been a pause in long-term contracting by utilities and thus sentiment has been driven by weakness in the small and illiquid spot market. Though we expect fits and starts, we continue to see a long-term positive backdrop for the nuclear space and see Cameco well positioned to benefit.

Chart Industries is a renewables equipment maker, engaged in the manufacturing of engineered equipment for the industrial gas, energy, and biomedical industries. The Company makes cryogenic equipment for LNG and other industrial gases, notably hydrogen. Following a strong fourth quarter share performance, concerns over tariffs and industrial slowdown weighed on shares during the quarter. In addition, while orders were strong in the fourth quarter, operational results fell short of expectations which given the company’s recent history of lowering guidance likely increased skepticism around management’s maintaining their 2025 outlook. We continue to see Chart Industries well positioned for a global LNG buildout and with strong backlog see good visibility to earnings growth and debt reduction over the next 12-18 months.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

Natural gas continued its march higher as early cold late last year followed through in January and February. A fast start to an LNG facility sustained prices later in the quarter as did what seems to be a relatively cautious approach among producers to add supply into these higher prices. While switching out of gas and into cheaper coal for power generation likely occurred, strong industrial and heating demand blunted the impact of that shift, which itself is limited relative to the past due to coal-plant shutdowns.

We added to the Fund’s positions here early in the quarter as gas support seems to have legs with LNG exports ramping and power generation demand doing the same for data centers and AI. European-focused gas suppliers, in contrast to U.S. producers, have vacillated with Russia/Ukraine peace prospects that could in turn open a perceived door for returning Russian gas to Europe.

While U.S. copper prices surged to a record high, surpassing mid- 2024’s previous peak, production delays, in turn contributing to the commodity strength, hurt our holdings. Further, copper tariff potential attracted front-run volumes into the U.S. that the market likely perceives as temporary price support. However, better Chinese demand and structural supply constraint points to more fundamental price strength, in the team’s view. We’ve added to the Fund’s copper holdings in expectation of this equity/commodity performance gap to close constructively.

Oil continues to wallow on the combination of economic-driven demand weakness, particularly from China, and Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries’ early-March decision to add volumes to the market starting in April. While the team previously lowered the Fund’s exposure to this typically large and diverse sleeve of the Fund, sentiment feels near a bottom. A tightening of Iranian sanctions could provide powerful price support.

Finally, a lack of detail from the Trump Administration regarding the future of renewables incentives, and dim near-term prospects for clarity on this issue, limits our solar exposure.

data.title: Ford's Dividend Under Threat As Ford Pro Margins Fall
data.description:
Ford (F) was able to beat the EPS and revenue estimates in the recent quarter. The company reported a mixed bag of numbers in key segments. The Model e segment which includes EV sales finally showed some signs of traction with EBIT loss of $0.8 billion compared to $1.3 billion loss in Q1 2024. However, its Ford Pro segment continues to show a decline in YoY EBIT margins. This is the main cash cow for the company, and any major decline in Ford Pro can have a big impact on the bottom line and the ability to maintain the dividends. The Ford Pro revenue in recent quarter was $15.2 billion compared to $18 billion in Q1 2024. The EBIT for Ford Pro also fell to $1.3 billion compared to $3 billion in the year-ago quarter. There has been a steady decline in revenue and EBIT numbers of Ford Pro for the past few quarters and this seems to be a bigger trend instead of some issue with tariffs. In the previous article, it was mentioned that multiple downward EPS revisions can start hurting the sentiment for the stock.

The macro trade situation continues to be volatile. It is highly likely that the second quarter GDP growth rate will be negative as tariffs headwind show their full impact in this quarter. We could also see negative GDP growth rate in the third quarter as the tariff situation between key players has not been resolved. This could be a major headwind for Ford and the ability of the company to continue paying the current dividends. The WSJ recently mentioned that "Ford's fat dividend could be a casualty of tariffs". It went further by saying that the question is not whether the dividend is cut but by how much. This report was before the latest earnings result. The massive decline in Ford Pro margins can be another big factor that forces the management to reevaluate the dividends.

The consensus EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027 is $1.54 which gives the stock a forward PE of 6.68. This is quite attractive but the forward EPS estimates have a very wide gap which shows massive uncertainty over future earnings potential. Despite the strong dividend and attractive PE multiple, the risks for Ford stock outweigh the returns, making it a sell.

Mixed Earnings Numbers In Recent Quarter

Ford was able to beat both EPS and revenue estimates in the recent quarter. The revenue surprise was $1.63 billion in the first quarter. Some of the demand was pulled ahead as customers wanted to get ahead of the tariffs. However, despite this beat, the company showed a negative trend in its Ford Pro segment. In Q1 2025, Ford Pro revenue came in at $15.2 billion with sales of 352,000 units. On the other hand, in Q1 2024 Ford Pro revenue was $18 billion with sales of 409,000. Over the last few quarters, there has been a steady decline in EBIT margin of Ford Pro compared to the year-ago quarter. It is essential for Ford to show a good EBIT margin trend in Ford Pro in order to invest this capital in other products.

Figure: Recent revenue and EBIT numbers for Ford Pro. Source: Company Filings

In Q4 2024, the EBIT margin of Ford Pro was 10% compared to 11.8% in the year-ago quarter. Similarly, in Q3 2024 the EBIT margin was 11.6% compared to 12% in the year-ago quarter. It would be essential for Ford to reverse this trend in order to maintain its earnings potential over the next few quarters as the company tries to expand the EV segment.

Figure: YoY drop in EBIT margins of Ford Pro. Source: Company Filings

The EV Business Finally Shows Some Silver Linings

The EBIT for the Model e segment was negative $0.8 billion in the recent quarter compared to negative $1.3 billion in Q1 2024. There has been improvement in units sold for this segment. In 2024, Ford had a cumulative negative EBIT of over $5 billion in the Model e segment. The company has been investing heavily to improve the product pipeline and sales of this segment. The recent quarter finally shows some green shoots in terms of units sold and EBIT. However, we would need to wait for another few quarters to see if the EBIT improvement in Model e is sustained. It will also take a few more quarters before this segment can actually deliver on the promises.

Figure: Improvement in EBIT of Model e in the recent quarter. Source: Company Filings

It should be noted that the competition in the EV business continues to heat up. Rivian (RIVN) will be launching its affordable R2 model in early 2026, followed soon by the R3 model. Most projections show that Rivian could grab a big chunk of EV market share with the affordable models. Rivian has also increased the revenue from Software and services segment. In the recent quarter, Rivian earned $318 million from Software business compared to overall revenue of $1.2 billion. Hence, Rivian’s software business had a revenue share of 26%. The software sales have a higher margin and are more recurring, which helps to reduce the volatility in overall revenue numbers.

Despite significantly lower resources, Rivian has been able to deliver a better traction from its software business. It is now launching its autonomous driving feature, which can be another major revenue contributor. It should be noted that Ford also has spent billions on autonomy projects. Some of them failed, like Argo AI. But the company still needs to deliver a product that can provide a steady revenue base.

Is It Likely To See A Dividend Cut?

The biggest question facing investors is the possibility of a dividend cut. The management has been mute on this question. However, we can see some clear signs which might force the management to cut the dividend. The tariff issue is certainly a major headwind. However, beyond the tariffs, we can see a steady decline in margins of Ford Pro as mentioned above. This is the main cash cow for the company. The company can reasonably afford the current payout but if there is a further dip in Ford Pro margins, we might see calls for a dividend adjustment.

Dividend safety gets an F grade according to Seeking Alpha Quant, as the forward dividend payout ratio is significantly more than the sector median. As mentioned below, GM has used its excess cash to cut outstanding stock while giving a more modest dividend yield.

Figure: Dividend safety gets an F grade. Source: Seeking Alpha

The macro situation for Ford continues to be very complicated. The company has already announced taking a tariff hit. But a bigger issue could be a slowdown in the economy. The GDP declined in the first quarter. This was prior to “liberation day” tariffs from early April. It is very likely that second quarter GDP results will also be negative. Despite some tariff reduction, the trade situation is still very volatile. This could lead to a further slowdown in GDP growth in the third quarter. The expectation of recession among CEOs has declined recently compared to April, but it is still quite high.

If we see three quarters of negative GDP growth, it could have a negative impact on the consumer sentiment. Ford’s management might also choose to be more prudent and limit dividend payouts if the macroeconomic situation turns negative. The dividend yield of Ford is quite good, but the future payouts are highly uncertain, which makes it difficult to recommend Ford stock as a dividend play.

Modest Valuation Multiple But Huge Risk

Ford is trading at only 7.3 times the consensus EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 and 6.68 times the consensus EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027. These are very modest numbers. It might be better if the management uses its cash for buybacks at this multiple, similar to GM (GM) which has reduced the outstanding stock significantly over the last few quarters.

Over the last three years, GM has reduced its outstanding shares by almost a third, compared to only 1% reduction by Ford. The rapid decrease in outstanding stock can help GM in improving its EPS momentum. GM's dividend yield is a lot lower than Ford, but the buybacks could provide a strong tailwind to EPS growth in the next few years for GM.

Despite the modest valuation multiple, Ford also shows a massive uncertainty in forward EPS projections. The lowest EPS projection for fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $1.01 while the highest EPS projection is $1.86. This is a very big gap for a mature company like Ford. Similarly, the EPS range for the fiscal year ending Dec 2027 is from $0.85 to $2.30. Hence, the highest EPS projection is almost 3X the lowest projection. With this level of gap, the risk is much greater than the reward potential, making the stock a Sell

Figure: Forward EPS estimates of Ford. Source: Seeking Alpha

Risks To The Thesis

The biggest risk to the thesis is the ability of Ford to deliver good traction in the Model e segment and improve the margins of Ford Pro business. The Model e has shown some green shoots, but it is far from a winner for Ford, especially after the company invested billions in this segment over the last few years. There have been some brand issues with Tesla (TSLA) which can give Ford a possible tailwind for its EV models. But there would need to be significant improvement in deliveries and margins for the Model e segment.

Ford Pro's margins have been cut in half in the first quarter. If there is a turnaround in margins for this segment, it can build a bullish momentum for the stock. However, Ford would be facing a tough macro condition in the near term, which might make it difficult to deliver a major improvement in margins of Ford Pro.

Investor Takeaway

Ford faces challenges in its Ford Pro segment with a declining YoY EBIT margin trend over the last few quarters. In the recent quarter, the EBIT for Ford Pro was only $1.3 billion, compared to $3 billion in the year-ago quarter. The Ford Model e segment is showing some progress as it reduces its EBIT losses and improves the sales. However, the EV segment is going to face massive competition in the near term as more affordable products are launched.

The macro headwinds can also make it difficult for Ford to maintain its high dividend payout. The company has already delayed its forward guidance, and we could see a lower-than-expected earnings guidance if these trade headwinds persist. The valuation multiple of Ford is very attractive, but it comes with a very large uncertainty for a mature company like Ford. There are few growth levers available with the company, while the potential for earnings to dip can increase in the next few quarters.

data.title: 10-Year U.S. Treasury Yield 'Fair Value' Estimate: 12 June 2025
data.description:
The market premium for the US Treasury yield remained mostly unchanged in May vs. a “fair value” estimate. As the 10-year yield continued to trade in a tight range last month, combined with virtually no change an model-based assessment of its fair value, today’s update for May is in line with the previous month’s analysis.

The current average monthly fair-value estimate is 3.72%, which remains moderately below the actual 10-year yield. In yesterday’s trading, the benchmark rate was 4.43% (June 11), a middling level vs. recent history. The spread for the market level vs. fair-value estimate edged up 70 basis points, which is also a middling level year to date. The fair value estimate is calculated as the mean via three models run by CapitalSpectator.com.

The market premium continued to hold in the 50-100 basis point range, which has prevailed so far in 2025. The relatively steady trend reflects uncertainty about how tariffs will influence inflation in the months ahead and how the Federal Reserve will change monetary policy, based on trade tensions. Meantime, investors are demanding a moderate yield premium over a theoretical fair value of the benchmark rate — a premium that’s fallen sharply vs. heights reached a few years ago, when inflation was surging.

Yesterday’s consumer inflation report for May, by comparison, was muted. The year-over-year change for CPI at the headline level ticked down to 2.4%, moderately above the Fed’s 2% inflation target. Core CPI, a more robust measure of the trend, held steady, which could be a sign that pricing pressure will remain “sticky” at a time when tariffs are only just starting to factor into prices.

“It was a very good report,” said Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody’s. “Basically, it says inflation has finally gotten back to the Federal Reserve’s annual inflation target.” He added: “I think it’s the calm before the inflation storm. This [report] still reflects the disinflation that began a few years ago and continued on through the month of May.”

“We’re not really seeing much of the pass-through, if some at all, from the tariffs,” advised RSM chief economist Joe Brusuelas. “That really did provide the much softer print than I expected or many analysts. … But don’t get too comfortable. When [companies] hike prices by 10% to 15%, it gets passed through eventually.”

data.title: Eastern Company: A Stronger Q1 Bodes Well For The Rest Of The Year
data.description:
Introduction

I have been keeping an eye on the Eastern Company (NASDAQ:EML) for several years now, but the share price hasn’t exactly performed well. The company has now also reoriented itself as it decided to sell the Big 3 Mold division in the third quarter of last year, and that division was recently included in the financial statements as a "discontinued operation" before Eastern sold the equipment, workforce and customer list of the ISBM division of Big 3 Precision mold portion of the company.

The Eastern Company will retain the other Big 3 brand, which focuses on Precision Products, which produces turnkey returnable packaging that is used in the assembly process of vehicles and aircraft. The Eberhard division focuses on the development and manufacturing of access and security hardware (like latches and locks) while Velvac manufactures a vision technology for OEMs (for instance mirror cameras and LED indicators incorporated in a truck’s side mirror) and is a provider of aftermarket components to the heavy-duty truck market in North America.

Decent earnings and a decent cash flow

In the first quarter of the current financial year, the company reported total revenue of $63.3M. That’s a 2% decrease compared to the same quarter of last year, but this is related to the Big 3 Mold business, which now has the status of a discontinued operation, which means its Q1 revenue of almost $4M was removed from Eastern’s consolidated top-line result. This means that on a like-for-like basis, Eastern’s revenue would have increased.

As the income statement below shows, the gross margin decreased while the operating profit also came in just over 5% lower than in the same quarter a year ago. This difference can once again be explained by the de-consolidation of Big 3 Mold, which generated an operating profit of approximately $0.2M in the first quarter of this year.

Looking at Eastern’s bottom-line result, the company reported net income of $1.9M from continuing operations and added approximately $37,000 in net profit from discontinued operations, resulting in a total consolidated net profit of $1.94M, a similar result compared to Q1 last year. The EPS in Q1 2025 was approximately $0.32 of which $0.31/share came from the continuing operations. That’s an increase of one cent per share due to the slightly higher net income in combination with a lower share count, as Eastern is buying back its own shares.

The income statement was also negatively impacted by a change in the carrying amount of right-of-use assets. As you can see below, Eastern Company reported a negative operating cash flow of $1.85M, but this is caused by a relatively hefty amount of cash required for its working capital elements. Adjusted for those working capital changes, the underlying operating cash flow was approximately $7.2M and roughly $7.1M after also taking the lease expenses into account.

The capex was very low at $0.85M, which means Eastern Company generated $6.25M in free cash flow on an underlying basis. That’s in excess of $1/share.

While the company’s underlying free cash flow was quite strong in the first quarter of the year, we can’t just extrapolate this to calculate the full-year free cash flow. Last year, for instance, the full-year capex was closer to $10M for the year while the normalized depreciation and amortization expenses are currently around $6M per year. This means we should reasonably expect the full-year capex to come in higher than the Q1 annualized capex.

Investment thesis

I currently have no position in Eastern Company, but I like the dedication of the new CEO who seems to determined to build Eastern out to a leader in its sector, with a focus on margins. Fortunately, the balance sheet remains pretty strong with a positive working capital position of in excess of $65M while the net debt level is just over $33M (excluding lease liabilities). This means the company has options (and time) to improve its financial performance, but the net income and net free cash flow on an underlying basis was already encouraging.

I’d like to see confirmation of the stronger than expected Q1 result in the next few quarters, so for now, I’m still just a "hold" on the stock.

data.title: FX: Risk Premium To Stay In The Dollar
data.description:
By Chris Turner

While we’re not expecting the dollar to collapse, we do think there are enough negative factors out there to keep it under pressure for the rest of the year.

Divestment alert

There has been a lot of talk about the ‘Sell America’ thesis and also ‘de-dollarization’. The two are linked, but the former, in our opinion, best describes the acute sell-off in US asset markets in April. The latter represents the much slower, secular trend away from a unipolar to a multipolar global financial system.

Relevant for both is the view that foreigners will end up holding fewer US assets than they have previously. A key focal point here is the US Treasury market and any evidence that foreigners have indeed been selling. There will therefore be interest in the US TIC data released on 18 June, which covers the month of April. Were central banks one of the key communities selling Treasuries that month?

The chart below shows foreign official holdings of Treasuries, drawing on data from both the US Treasury TIC report and the Fed's custody holdings. The latter is a subset of the former, but they typically profile quite well. The more frequent release from the Fed shows a $20bn decline in foreign official holdings since April and suggests the April TIC data could well support the foreign divestment hypothesis – a dollar negative.

Snapshot of foreign central bank holdings of Treasuries

The macro is dollar negative too

We expect tariff and US policy uncertainty to keep a risk premium in the dollar this year. One such policy on the market’s radar is Section 899 of the tax bill, which threatens a new withholding tax on foreign investors whose governments are deemed to run discriminatory tax regimes. Should that go into law, expect the dollar to turn quite offered into year-end as global investors optimize portfolios ahead of its introduction in 2026.

But dollar weakness will not be entirely down to a risk premium. The softer US growth trajectory and the restart of the Fed's easing cycle later this year should keep the dollar offered. Fed rate cuts, in particular, can reduce dollar hedging costs and make it easier to increase hedge ratios on holdings of US assets. On this subject, we suspect that current high dollar interest rates are discouraging some of that activity.

Equally, the eurozone story should start to look a little more attractive through 2026 as the benefits of this year’s fiscal stimulus decisions bear fruit. Importantly, we’re looking for eurozone money market rates to start picking up in late 2026 ahead of what could be an ECB hike in early 2027. We expect EUR/USD to trade towards the upper end of a 1.15-1.20 range at that stage. And that’s why this month we are nudging our EUR/USD forecast profile slightly higher.
